# `.lca` Fileformat - especially `"Samples": `

I have the right code path now; I’m decoding the exact base64 payload and counting the run lengths so I can map it directly to low and high sample regions.

Ran terminal command:  python3 - <<'PY'
import base64
s = ''''''
raw = base64.b64decode(s)
print('length', len(raw))
# summarize runs
runs=[]
if raw:
    cur=raw[0]; count=1
    for b in raw[1:]:
        if b==cur:
            count+=1
        else:
            runs.append((cur,count))
            cur=b; count=1
    runs.append((cur,count))
print('unique', sorted(set(raw)))
print('runs', runs)
print('zeros', raw.count(0), 'ones', raw.count(1))
PY

Read [](https://github.com/gusmanb/logicanalyzer/tree/master/Software/LogicAnalyzer/SharedDriver/LogicAnalyzerDriver.cs#318-318), lines 318 to 470

The decode surfaced one unexpected detail: this payload is mostly `0`, with one block of `1`, but two later blocks are `16`, not `1`. I’m checking whether anything besides the normal bit-extraction path can populate `Samples`, because the main driver path should only emit `0` or `1`.

Searched for regex `Samples\s*=\s*` (`**/Software/LogicAnalyzer/**`), 100 results

Searched for regex `new AnalyzerChannel|AnalyzerChannel\s*\{` (`**/Software/LogicAnalyzer/**`), 9 results

Searched for regex `class LACEnvelope|LACEnvelope` (`**/Software/LogicAnalyzer/**`), 3 results

I found other paths that can rewrite `Samples` after capture inside the GUI, so I’m checking those transforms. That will tell us whether `16` is a meaningful logic level or just an artifact of a post-processing operation.

Read [](https://github.com/gusmanb/logicanalyzer/tree/master/Software/LogicAnalyzer/LogicAnalyzer/Controls/SampleViewer.axaml.cs#120-120), lines 120 to 170

Read [](https://github.com/gusmanb/logicanalyzer/tree/master/Software/LogicAnalyzer/LogicAnalyzer/MainWindow.axaml.cs#548-548), lines 548 to 735

Read [](https://github.com/gusmanb/logicanalyzer/tree/master/Software/LogicAnalyzer/LogicAnalyzer/MainWindow.axaml.cs#1018-1018), lines 1018 to 1042

The viewer code treats any nonzero sample as logic high, which explains why a `16` block would still display as high. I’m checking whether the DAQ/import path preserves a bit mask value instead of normalizing it to `1`.

Searched for regex `DAQ|Daq|Data Acquisition|Acquisition` (`**/Software/LogicAnalyzer/**`), no results

Searched for regex `channel\.Samples\s*=\s*.*16|channel\.Samples\s*=\s*.*mask|!= 0 \? \(byte\)1 : \(byte\)0|lastSample != 0` (`**/Software/LogicAnalyzer/**`), 6 results

Read [](https://github.com/gusmanb/logicanalyzer/tree/master/Software/LogicAnalyzer/TerminalCapture/Classes/LACEnvelope.cs)

I have enough to explain the encoding now. I’m doing one last compact summary pass on the decoded bytes so I can give you the exact sample regions in plain terms.

Ran terminal command:  python3 - <<'PY'
import base64
s = ''''''
raw = base64.b64decode(s)
# raw runs
runs=[]
cur=raw[0]; count=1
for b in raw[1:]:
    if b==cur: count+=1
    else:
        runs.append((cur,count))
        cur=b; count=1
runs.append((cur,count))
print('raw runs:', runs)
# merged logic levels
logic=[]
cur = 1 if raw[0] != 0 else 0
count=1
for b in raw[1:]:
    v = 1 if b != 0 else 0
    if v==cur: count+=1
    else:
        logic.append((cur,count))
        cur=v; count=1
logic.append((cur,count))
print('logic runs:', logic)
PY

The value is not text. It is the JSON serialization of a byte array.

In this codebase, Samples is a byte array on each channel in AnalyzerChannel.cs, and session files are saved with Newtonsoft.Json in FileOperations.cs. Newtonsoft serializes a byte array as Base64, so a long string like "AAAAAAAA..." is just compact storage for raw sample bytes.

On the normal capture path, each stored sample is supposed to be either 0 or 1. That happens here:
LogicAnalyzerDriver.cs
and the same logic exists in the UI path here:
MainWindow.axaml.cs

Your exact Base64 string decodes to 4713 sample bytes with these raw runs:

1. 0 repeated 511 times
2. 1 repeated 410 times
3. 0 repeated 1473 times
4. 16 repeated 449 times
5. 0 repeated 1478 times
6. 16 repeated 362 times

The viewer treats any nonzero byte as logic high, not just 1:
SampleViewer.axaml.cs

So the waveform meaning is:

1. Low for 511 samples
2. High for 410 samples
3. Low for 1473 samples
4. High for 449 samples
5. Low for 1478 samples
6. High for 362 samples

What the Base64 fragments mean:

1. "A" heavy regions decode to 0x00 bytes, so those are low samples.
2. "AQEBAQ..." decodes to repeated 0x01 bytes, so those are high samples.
3. "EBAQ..." decodes to repeated 0x10 bytes, which is decimal 16. In this app that is still interpreted as high because the code checks nonzero, not equal to 1.

The interesting part is the 16 values. They are not expected from the normal extraction path, which should normalize highs to 1. Byte value 16 is exactly the bit mask for bit 4, meaning channel 5 if channels are zero-based. So the most likely explanation is that this capture was produced by a path that preserved the channel mask value 0x10 instead of collapsing it to 1. Functionally in the UI it still means high.

So the short answer is: your capture represents a digital pattern of low, high, low, high, low, high, and the long string is only the Base64 encoding of that byte sequence. The only anomaly is that two high sections were stored as 16 instead of 1, which still displays as high but suggests a masked-value path somewhere upstream.

If you want, I can trace where that 16 is getting introduced and point to the exact DAQ/import path that is writing masked values instead of normalized 0 and 1.
